title: What Is a Roth IRA? A Complete Beginner's Guide for 2026
excerpt: Learn what a Roth IRA is, how it works, and why it might be the best retirement account for building tax-free wealth.
relatedKeywords: how does a roth ira work
content:
## The Retirement Account That Lets You Keep More of Your Money

If you have started researching retirement savings, you have probably come across the term "Roth IRA." It is one of the most powerful tools for building long-term wealth, yet many people do not fully understand what makes it special or how to use it effectively.

A Roth IRA is a type of individual retirement account that offers a unique tax advantage: you contribute money that has already been taxed, and in return, all your investment growth and withdrawals in retirement are completely tax-free.

This guide will explain exactly how a Roth IRA works, who should use one, and how to get started. If you are new to investing, you may also want to read our guide on [how to start investing with 100 dollars](/blog/how-to-start-investing-with-100-dollars).

## How a Roth IRA Works

To understand why a Roth IRA is so valuable, you need to understand how taxes work with retirement accounts.

### The Basic Concept

With a Roth IRA, you pay taxes now to avoid paying taxes later. Here is the simple version:

1. You earn money at your job and pay income tax on it.
2. You contribute some of that after-tax money to your Roth IRA.
3. Your money grows through investments over the years.
4. When you withdraw the money in retirement, you pay zero taxes on any of it.

The key benefit is that all your investment gains, which could be substantial over decades thanks to [compound interest](/blog/understanding-compound-interest-beginners-guide), are never taxed. If you contribute $6,000 and it grows to $60,000 over 30 years, you can withdraw the entire $60,000 without owing a penny in taxes.

### Roth IRA vs. Traditional IRA

The main alternative to a Roth IRA is a Traditional IRA, which works in reverse:

**Traditional IRA:**
- Contribute pre-tax money (get a tax deduction now)
- Money grows tax-deferred
- Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement

**Roth IRA:**
- Contribute after-tax money (no tax deduction now)
- Money grows tax-free
- Pay zero taxes when you withdraw in retirement

Which is better depends on whether you expect to be in a higher or lower tax bracket in retirement. For most young people, a Roth IRA is the better choice because tax rates tend to increase over time, and you likely earn less now than you will later. Setting [financial goals in your 20s](/blog/financial-goals-for-your-20s) often starts with choosing the right retirement account.

## Who Can Contribute to a Roth IRA?

Not everyone is eligible to contribute to a Roth IRA. There are income limits that determine whether you can contribute the full amount, a reduced amount, or nothing at all.

### Income Limits for 2026

For single filers:
- Full contribution allowed: Modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) under $146,000
- Partial contribution allowed: MAGI between $146,000 and $161,000
- No contribution allowed: MAGI over $161,000

For married filing jointly:
- Full contribution allowed: MAGI under $230,000
- Partial contribution allowed: MAGI between $230,000 and $240,000
- No contribution allowed: MAGI over $240,000

If you earn too much to contribute directly, there is a legal workaround called a "backdoor Roth IRA" that involves contributing to a Traditional IRA and then converting it. This is more complex and may require professional guidance.

### Contribution Limits

For 2026, you can contribute up to $7,000 per year to a Roth IRA. If you are 50 or older, you can contribute an additional $1,000 as a "catch-up" contribution, bringing your total to $8,000.

These limits apply across all your IRA accounts combined. If you have both a Roth IRA and a Traditional IRA, your total contributions to both cannot exceed $7,000 (or $8,000 if you are 50+).

### You Must Have Earned Income

To contribute to a Roth IRA, you (or your spouse if filing jointly) must have earned income from a job or self-employment. Investment income, rental income, and Social Security do not count.

Your contribution cannot exceed your earned income for the year. If you only earned $3,000, you can only contribute $3,000 to your Roth IRA.

## The Biggest Benefits of a Roth IRA

There are several reasons why financial experts often recommend Roth IRAs, especially for younger investors.

### Tax-Free Growth

This is the main attraction. Every dollar your investments earn inside a Roth IRA is yours to keep. If your account grows from $50,000 to $500,000 over your working career, you will not owe any taxes on that $450,000 gain.

In a regular brokerage account, you would owe capital gains taxes on those profits. In a Traditional IRA or 401(k), you would owe income taxes when you withdraw. With a Roth IRA, you owe nothing.

### Flexible Withdrawals

Unlike other retirement accounts, a Roth IRA gives you flexibility:

**Contributions can be withdrawn anytime:** You can take out the money you contributed (not the earnings) at any age, for any reason, without penalties or taxes. This makes a Roth IRA a backup emergency fund.

**No required minimum distributions:** Traditional IRAs and 401(k)s force you to start taking money out at age 73. Roth IRAs have no such requirement. You can leave the money to grow for as long as you live, making them excellent for estate planning.

### Tax Diversification in Retirement

Having multiple types of accounts (Roth, Traditional, taxable) gives you flexibility in retirement. If tax rates rise, you can draw from your Roth IRA. If you are in a low-tax year, you can draw from Traditional accounts. This optionality has real value.

### Protection from Future Tax Increases

No one knows what tax rates will look like in 30 or 40 years. By paying taxes now at current known rates, you lock in your tax treatment. If rates go up significantly, you will be glad you used a Roth.

## How to Open and Fund a Roth IRA

Getting started with a Roth IRA is straightforward. Here is the step-by-step process.

### Step 1: Choose Where to Open Your Account

You can open a Roth IRA at most brokerages, banks, and investment companies. For a detailed walkthrough, see our guide on [how to open a brokerage account](/blog/how-to-open-brokerage-account-beginners-guide). The best options for most people are:

**Fidelity:** No account fees, no minimums, excellent fund selection, great customer service.

**Charles Schwab:** Similar to Fidelity with strong research tools and support.

**Vanguard:** Known for low-cost index funds. Slightly less polished interface but excellent fund options.

All three are reputable, have no account fees, and offer commission-free trading. The differences are minor, so do not overthink this choice.

### Step 2: Open the Account

Go to your chosen brokerage's website and look for "Open an Account." Select "Roth IRA" as the account type.

You will need to provide personal information including your name, address, Social Security number, date of birth, and employment information. The process takes about 10-15 minutes.

### Step 3: Link Your Bank Account

Connect your checking or savings account so you can transfer money. Most brokerages verify this through small test deposits.

### Step 4: Make Your First Contribution

Transfer money from your bank to your Roth IRA. You can contribute a lump sum or set up automatic monthly contributions. Many people contribute $583 per month to max out the $7,000 annual limit.

### Step 5: Invest the Money

This is crucial: just depositing money is not enough. Money sitting in your Roth IRA as cash does not grow. You need to actually invest it.

For most people, the best choice is a low-cost total stock market [index fund](/blog/index-funds-for-beginners) or target-date retirement fund. These provide instant diversification and require minimal maintenance.

## What to Invest In Inside Your Roth IRA

Once you have funded your Roth IRA, you need to put that money to work. Here are the most common options.

### Target-Date Retirement Funds

These are "set it and forget it" funds designed for people retiring around a specific year. If you plan to retire around 2060, you would choose a Target 2060 fund.

The fund automatically adjusts its mix of stocks and bonds over time, becoming more conservative as you approach retirement. This is the simplest option and works well for most people.

### Index Funds

If you want slightly more control, you can build a simple portfolio with index funds:

**Total Stock Market Fund:** Covers the entire U.S. stock market. Examples include VTI (Vanguard) or FSKAX (Fidelity).

**Total International Fund:** Covers stocks outside the U.S. Examples include VXUS (Vanguard) or FTIHX (Fidelity).

**Total Bond Market Fund:** Covers U.S. bonds for stability. Examples include BND (Vanguard) or FXNAX (Fidelity).

A common allocation for young investors is 80% stocks (split between U.S. and international) and 20% bonds, adjusting toward more bonds as you age.

### Individual Stocks

You can buy individual stocks in a Roth IRA, but this is riskier and requires more knowledge. If you want to pick stocks, consider limiting it to a small portion of your portfolio (10-20%) and keeping the rest in diversified funds.

## Common Roth IRA Rules and Considerations

There are some important rules to understand before you start.

### The Five-Year Rule

To withdraw earnings tax-free, your Roth IRA must be at least five years old. This clock starts on January 1 of the year you make your first contribution.

This rule typically only matters if you are close to retirement when you open your account. For young investors, it is rarely an issue.

### Early Withdrawal Rules

Before age 59.5, different rules apply to contributions versus earnings:

**Contributions:** Can always be withdrawn tax-free and penalty-free.

**Earnings:** Withdrawing before 59.5 generally incurs a 10% penalty plus income taxes. However, there are exceptions for first-time home purchases (up to $10,000), qualified education expenses, and certain hardships.

### Contribution Deadlines

You have until the tax filing deadline (typically April 15) to make contributions for the previous year. For example, you can make 2026 contributions until April 15, 2027.

This gives you flexibility if you are short on funds at year-end but want to max out your contribution.

## Roth IRA vs. Other Retirement Accounts

How does a Roth IRA compare to other options?

### Roth IRA vs. 401(k)

A 401(k) is an employer-sponsored retirement account. Key differences:

- 401(k)s have higher contribution limits ($23,000 in 2026 vs. $7,000 for IRAs)
- 401(k)s may offer employer matching (free money)
- Roth IRAs have more investment choices
- Roth IRAs offer more withdrawal flexibility

**Best approach:** If your employer offers a 401(k) match, contribute enough to get the full match first. Then max out your Roth IRA. After that, return to your 401(k) if you can save more.

### Roth IRA vs. Roth 401(k)

Some employers offer a Roth 401(k), which combines features of both:

- After-tax contributions like a Roth IRA
- Higher limits like a traditional 401(k)
- Subject to required minimum distributions (unlike Roth IRA)

If your employer offers matching on a Roth 401(k), take advantage of it. You can still have both a Roth 401(k) and a Roth IRA.

### Roth IRA vs. Taxable Brokerage Account

A regular brokerage account has no contribution limits or income restrictions, but you pay taxes on dividends and capital gains each year.

Use a Roth IRA first for retirement savings. Use a taxable account for money you might need before retirement or after you have maxed out tax-advantaged accounts.

## Should You Choose a Roth IRA?

A Roth IRA is usually the right choice if:

- You are in a relatively low tax bracket now
- You expect your income to increase significantly
- You are young and have decades for tax-free growth
- You value flexibility in when and how you withdraw
- You want to leave money to heirs tax-efficiently

A Traditional IRA might be better if:

- You are in a high tax bracket now and expect to be in a lower bracket in retirement
- You need the upfront tax deduction
- You earn too much for Roth IRA contributions and do not want the complexity of a backdoor Roth

For most people under 40, a Roth IRA is the better choice. The tax-free growth over decades is simply too valuable to pass up.

## Getting Started: Your Action Plan

Here is what to do this week:

1. Check if you are eligible based on your income.
2. Choose a brokerage (Fidelity, Schwab, or Vanguard all work well).
3. Open a Roth IRA account online (takes 10-15 minutes).
4. Link your bank account.
5. Make your first contribution, even if it is just $50 to start.
6. Invest the money in a target-date fund or total market index fund.
7. Set up automatic monthly contributions if possible.

The hardest part is taking that first step. Once your Roth IRA is open and automated, it essentially runs on autopilot while building your tax-free retirement fund.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### What is a Roth IRA?

A Roth IRA is a retirement account where you contribute money you have already paid taxes on. Your investments grow tax-free, and withdrawals in retirement are also tax-free. The 2024 contribution limit is $7,000 per year ($8,000 if you are 50 or older).

### Is a Roth IRA better than a 401k?

They serve different purposes. A 401k offers tax deductions now and often includes employer matching (free money). A Roth IRA offers tax-free withdrawals in retirement and more investment options. Ideally, contribute enough to your 401k to get the full employer match, then fund a Roth IRA with additional savings.

### How much should I put in a Roth IRA?

Contribute as much as you can up to the annual limit ($7,000 in 2024). If you cannot afford the maximum, even $50-100/month is valuable because of compound growth over decades. Automating monthly contributions makes it easier to stay consistent.

### Can I withdraw money from a Roth IRA early?

You can withdraw your contributions (not earnings) at any time with no penalty or taxes. Earnings withdrawn before age 59.5 may face taxes and a 10% penalty, with some exceptions (first home purchase, education, disability). This flexibility makes a Roth IRA more accessible than most retirement accounts.

## Sources

- [IRS: Roth IRAs](https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/roth-iras)
- [SEC: Investor.gov - IRAs](https://www.investor.gov/introduction-investing/getting-started/saving-and-investing/individual-retirement-accounts-iras)

## The Bottom Line

A Roth IRA is one of the most powerful wealth-building tools available to individual investors. The combination of tax-free growth, flexible withdrawals, and no required distributions makes it uniquely valuable.

If you are eligible and have not opened one yet, there is no better time than now. Even small contributions add up over decades, and every year you delay is a year of tax-free growth you cannot get back.

Open your Roth IRA today, and learn [how to set financial goals](/blog/how-to-set-financial-goals) to build a complete plan for your future.
